claim: Self-evaluation is a trap for LLM agents; adversarial evaluator agents with separate context windows and harsh prompts work better than self-critical loops
actionable: Build agent systems using a generator-evaluator pattern with separate roles and adversarial pressure rather than having agents review their own work in a single context window.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Context compaction does not prevent coherence drift; structured handoffs and clean context boundaries are the solution to maintain coherence across long agent runs
actionable: Use explicit file-based state handoffs and context resets between phases rather than relying on lossy summarization to maintain agent coherence over multi-hour runs.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Decompose long-running agent work into testable sprint contracts: generator and evaluator negotiate acceptance criteria before execution, preventing cascading design errors
actionable: Have the generator and evaluator agents write markdown contracts specifying what done means before starting implementation, then evaluate against that contract not the original vague spec.
Have generator and evaluator negotiate acceptance criteria in markdown before any code is written, ensuring both agents agree on specific testable assertions.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Subjective quality (design taste, craft) can be graded by LLMs if you specify rubrics with 4-6 weighted criteria and calibrate with few-shot examples on reference sites
actionable: Create detailed rubrics with weighted criteria (design, originality, craft, functionality) and provide 3-5 annotated examples showing what good/bad looks like, then use an evaluator agent to grade against this rubric.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Reading traces is the primary debugging loop for long-running agents—empathize with the model by imagining its perspective (vision-based navigation with 10-second snapshots, no state retention) to spot blindspots
actionable: When debugging agent failures, manually read full transcripts line-by-line and simulate the agent's experience (limited vision, no context) to understand why it made unexpected choices.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Models improve faster than harnesses, so design harnesses for the next-generation model, not the current one, to avoid rework when the frontier advances
actionable: When building harness components, ask whether they'll be necessary in 1-2 model generations; if not, simplify now to reduce technical debt when models improve.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Generator-evaluator systems can pivot and restart from scratch when stuck, avoiding the patch-iteration trap of single-agent loops that re-optimize local minima
actionable: Empower the evaluator agent to recommend full restarts when it detects the generator is stuck on a single criterion; don't force continuation on a failing design.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Use file-system-based shared state over context windows for multi-hour agent runs; models overwrite markdown files less reliably than JSON, so prefer JSON for persistent facts
actionable: Store persistent state (feature lists, progress, logs) in JSON files rather than markdown or context variables for long-running multi-agent systems.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Planner agent should spec only high-level workflow (sprints, features) not granular technical details; let generator-evaluator iterate on specifics to avoid cascading planning errors
actionable: Have the planner write a 1-2 page spec of major features/sprints, then hand off to generator-evaluator to negotiate technical contracts without planner re-entering the loop.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Opus 4.6 removed need for multi-session resets; single continuous session with server-side compaction is now sufficient for 12+ hour coherent work if model is context-aware
actionable: For Opus 4.6+, simplify harness by using continuous single sessions instead of forcing context resets; rely on server-side compaction and the model's built-in context awareness.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Claude models exhibit context anxiety near the end of context window (hurried completion); newer models like Opus 4.6 don't show this as much post-training
actionable: Monitor whether your Claude model version exhibits rushing behavior near context limits; if using older models, insert explicit prompts to slow down as token limit approaches.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Early Claude models struggled with verifying their own output—would mark half-baked features as done or build buttons with no backend; evaluator pattern solves this via external judgment
actionable: Never rely on a single model pass for judgment on code quality; always use an external evaluator to verify implementation against acceptance criteria.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson

claim: Use Playwright MCP or Claude for Chrome MCP for browser control in long-running agents; allows evaluator to test live web apps (not just snapshots) and click/navigate/zoom
actionable: For web app evaluation, integrate Playwright or Claude for Chrome MCP into your evaluator agent so it can interact with the running app, not just read HTML.
source_title: Anthropic Workshop: Build Agents That Run for Hours — Ash Prabaker & Andrew Wilson
